structured_choices.data_source: Communication logs (emails and chat messages) for ~10,000 employees over 24 months from company production systems. Includes timestamped messages, sender/recipient metadata, message length, and thread participation. Ground truth: HR records of employee departure dates and employment status at data collection cutoff.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: employee departed the company (1) within a defined prediction window (e.g., 90 days following observation period end) vs. remained employed (0). Turnover events are determined from official HR termination records. Observation period is locked at T=0 to T=720 days; prediction window is T=720 to T=810 days. This prevents leakage of post-departure communication changes into features.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by department and employment tenure cohorts. Training: employees and their communication logs from days 0–450. Validation: days 451–600. Test: days 601–720 (observation period only; labels determined post-720). This temporal boundary prevents the model from observing communication patterns that occur after or near departure, which would be unrealistic in deployment. Stratification ensures each split has comparable departmental composition and tenure distributions, addressing potential confounding by organization structure.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing (scaling, tokenization, vocabulary construction, missing value imputation for communication metadata) is fit exclusively on the training set. Fitted transformers (e.g., TF-IDF vectorizer, scaler parameters, imputation statistics) are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents information leakage from validation/test distributions into feature engineering.
structured_choices.features: RNN model: sequenced communication features constructed as follows: for each employee, extract all timestamped messages in order within the observation window; compute rolling statistics (message count per week, average message length, recipient diversity, response time to messages, participation in threads) in non-overlapping 2-week windows. These form a sequence of 26 temporal snapshots. Additionally include static baseline features (tenure, department, job level, prior communication baseline) fed as initial state. Baseline logistic regression model: static features only—tenure, department, job level, average communication frequency (pre-computed once across observation period), recipient count, and message length percentiles. Feature engineering for both models uses only data strictly prior to the prediction window to avoid label leakage.
structured_choices.model: RNN model: bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, trained on sequences of 26 timesteps (2-week aggregated communication features). Output layer: single sigmoid unit for binary classification. Loss: focal loss or weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance. Baseline model: logistic regression with L2 regularization, fit on static feature set with class weight balancing.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression with static demographic and averaged communication features. This baseline is given equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: both L2 penalty strength and class weights are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same primary metric. The baseline uses the same label definition, split strategy, and evaluation conditions as the RNN. This ensures a fair comparison: the hypothesis claims RNN gains come from temporal modeling, not from better tuning or information access.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Precision at a fixed recall operating point (e.g., top 20% of employees by departure risk, or recall=0.70). Precision is operationally motivated: HR wants to identify high-risk employees to intervene, and false positives (flagging stayers) waste intervention resources. We fix recall to ensure the comparison is not confounded by threshold choice; both models are evaluated at the same sensitivity to turnover events. Alternative framing: area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC), which is insensitive to class imbalance and emphasizes performance in the positive (turnover) class.
secondary_metrics: Recall at the same operating point (to verify the RNN is not simply gaming precision by being more conservative).
F1-score (harmonic mean, provides a balanced view).
Calibration: expected calibration error (ECE) and Brier score, to assess whether predicted probabilities are trustworthy for HR decision-making.
ROC-AUC (for completeness; less operationally relevant given class imbalance, but standard reporting).
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (days 451–600) using the primary metric (precision at fixed recall). A grid search or random search explores: for RNN, hidden unit count (32, 64, 128), dropout rate (0.1–0.5), learning rate (1e-4 to 1e-2); for logistic regression, L2 penalty (1e-4 to 1e) and class weight ratios. The model (and hyperparameters) with the best validation primary metric is then evaluated once on the held-out test set. No re-tuning on test data.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is used exactly once, after model selection is complete. Test set results are reported and form the final claim. The test set is never used for hyperparameter tuning, feature engineering, or model selection. If additional analysis is desired (e.g., error analysis, subgroup performance), a small auxiliary hold-out set can be used; the primary test set remains pristine for the final comparison.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Economic/policy confounds are addressed by stratifying the validation and test splits by time quartile and company business unit to ensure both splits see similar macro conditions. If a company-wide event (e.g., layoff, reorganization) occurs during the observation period, a sensitivity analysis repeats the experiment excluding that period or stratifying strictly before/after the event. Department-level heterogeneity is handled by stratifying splits by department and reporting performance separately by department to detect if the RNN advantage is uniform or concentrated. Temporal confounds (e.g., seasonal hiring/attrition patterns) are implicitly captured by the RNN's temporal modeling; the comparison between RNN and static regression tests whether this temporal information adds predictive value beyond static features. The experiment does not establish causality (whether communication patterns cause turnover or vice versa), only predictive association.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Employee Turnover Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy and Label Definition

The experiment uses communication logs (email and chat messages) for approximately 10,000 employees over a 24-month period, sourced from the company's production systems. Each record includes a timestamp, sender, recipient(s), message length, and thread metadata. Ground truth is obtained from official HR termination records.

To prevent leakage of post-departure communication changes, we define a strict temporal boundary: the observation window runs from T=0 to T=720 days. The label is assigned based on whether the employee departed within a subsequent 90-day prediction window (T=720 to T=810 days). This ensures that all features are constructed from behavior strictly before the outcome occurs—the only realistic scenario for deployment.

### 2. Temporal Split with Stratification

We use a temporal split to preserve the sequential structure and avoid future information leaking into training:
- **Training:** Days 0–450 (approximately 60% of observation window)
- **Validation:** Days 451–600 (approximately 25%)
- **Test:** Days 601–720 (approximately 15%, held until final evaluation)

This temporal boundary is critical: the RNN learns from earlier communication sequences and is asked to predict on held-out later periods. This mirrors how the model would be deployed—trained on historical data, applied to future employees.

Within each split, we stratify by department and tenure cohort to ensure balanced representation. This controls for confounding by organizational structure (e.g., different departments may have different baseline turnover rates) and addresses the risk that economic downturns or policy changes affect some departments differently.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**For the RNN:** Communication logs are converted into sequences. For each employee, we compute rolling 2-week statistics: message count, average message length, recipient diversity, response latency, and thread participation. This produces 26 timesteps per employee (one per 2-week window across the 720-day observation period). These sequences form the primary input. Static features (tenure, department, job level) are provided as initial context but are not predictive on their own.

**For the baseline logistic regression:** We use only static features—tenure, department, job level, and pre-computed aggregates of communication frequency (e.g., average messages per week, percentiles of message length, distinct recipient count). These aggregates are computed once across the entire observation window, preserving no temporal structure.

Critically, all preprocessing (vocabulary construction for message content if used, scaling, imputation) is fit only on the training set. Fitted transformers are applied to validation and test without refitting, preventing data leakage.

### 4. Model Specification and Baseline

**RNN model:** A bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, trained on the 26-timestep sequences. Output: sigmoid unit for binary classification. Loss function: focal loss or weighted cross-entropy to handle class imbalance (fewer departures than stays).

**Baseline model:** Logistic regression with L2 regularization fit on static features. This baseline is deliberately simple but is tuned with equivalent care: both the L2 penalty strength and class weight ratio are selected via grid search on the validation set. This ensures the comparison tests whether temporal modeling adds value, not whether one model received better engineering.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operational Alignment

**Primary metric:** Precision at fixed recall (e.g., recall=0.70, meaning we identify 70% of future departures). Alternatively, we report the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC).

Precision is chosen because HR's operational goal is to identify employees likely to leave so they can intervene. False positives (flagging employees who stay) waste intervention resources. Fixing recall ensures both models are evaluated at the same sensitivity; the comparison hinges on whether the RNN produces fewer false alarms.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall, F1-score, calibration (expected calibration error, Brier score), and ROC-AUC for completeness. Calibration is important because HR will likely use predicted probabilities to triage interventions.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Handling

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set only. We search over RNN hidden unit counts, dropout rates, and learning rates; for logistic regression, penalty strength and class weights. The model with the best validation precision (at fixed recall) is selected.

The test set is touched exactly once: after model selection is complete, we evaluate both the tuned RNN and tuned logistic regression on the held-out test data and report the final comparison.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

We stratify splits by business unit and time quartile to ensure both splits experience similar macro conditions, reducing confounding by economic or company-wide policy changes. We report performance separately by department to check whether the RNN advantage is uniform or heterogeneous. We do not establish causality—only whether temporal communication patterns improve prediction over static features.
